modules.title: The golden rules of DIY
modules.html:
Before you start

The golden rules of DIY

Homemade care can transform your hair — or damage it if you improvise. These 6 rules are non-negotiable.

1 · Hygiene first

Clean bowls, whisks and hands. A homemade treatment has zero preservatives: any bacteria multiplies fast.

2 · Small batches, used quickly

A fruit or yogurt mask is used the same day (48 h max in the fridge). A water-based spray keeps 1 week in the fridge. Pure oil blends keep for months.

3 · Patch test

New ingredient? Apply a dab on your inner wrist, wait 24 h. Redness or itching = skip it.

4 · Essential oils are ALWAYS diluted

Never neat on skin: 1–2 drops per tablespoon of carrier oil, no more. Avoid during pregnancy unless your doctor approves.

5 · One new thing at a time

Test each recipe alone for 2 weeks before adding another. Otherwise you will never know what worked (that is what your tracker sheet is for).

6 · DIY complements — it does not replace everything

Some things simply do not exist in kitchen form: hydrolyzed proteins small enough to enter the hair shaft, safe preservatives, UV filters. We will always tell you honestly when that is the case.

modules.title: 12 ingredients, zero waste
modules.html:
Your hair pantry

12 ingredients, zero waste

The whole book relies on these simple ingredients — from the market, the supermarket or the health-food store.

IngredientWhat it doesTypeGood to know
Shea butterSeals in moisture, protects from cold and frictionSealantMelt it in a water bath, never on high in the microwave.
Coconut oilPenetrates the strand and limits protein loss during washingPenetrating emollientIdeal as a pre-poo. Solidifies under 75°F — warm it between your palms.
Olive oilSoftens, helps detanglingEmollientThe one in your kitchen works (extra virgin).
Castor oilThickens the feel of hair, protects the endsHeavy sealantNo, it does not “make hair grow” — but it reduces breakage, so you keep your length.
Jojoba oilClose to natural sebum, soothes the scalpLight emollientThe best fit for scalp care.
Aloe vera (gel/juice)Hydrates, soothes, lightens mixturesHydratorChoose 99%+ pure with no alcohol, or use the plant.
HoneyDraws water into the hair and holds it thereHumectantAlways mixed, never neat (too sticky).
Vegetable glycerinPowerful humectantHumectantCareful in very dry air (harmattan, heated winters): it can reverse its effect. See p. 4.
AvocadoNourishing fats that softenEmollientVery ripe and blended smooth, or say hello to bits at rinse time.
Banana + yogurtCoats and softens the hair surfaceSoftenerHonestly: food proteins do not penetrate like the hydrolyzed proteins in products. Real softness, yes — deep repair, no.
Rhassoul (clay)Cleanses gently without strippingCleanserHealth-food stores or Middle-Eastern shops, very affordable.
Apple cider vinegarTightens the cuticle, revives shineAcidifierAlways diluted (1 tbsp per cup of water).

modules.title: Adapt every recipe to YOUR hair
modules.html:
How to use this book

Adapt every recipe to YOUR hair

The same recipe behaves differently depending on your porosity. Every page has two adaptation boxes — here is the logic.

Low porosity (product “sits on top”)
• Tight cuticles: water and treatments struggle to get in.
• Your ally: gentle heat (heat cap, warm towel) to lift the cuticle.
• Prefer light textures, avoid piling on heavy butters.
• Apply on very damp, warm hair.
High porosity (drinks it in… then leaks)
• Open cuticles: water gets in fast and escapes just as fast.
• Your ally: sealing — always finish with shea or a heavy oil.
• A diluted vinegar rinse helps close the cuticle.
• Rich treatments are your friends.
Glycerin and climate (read me before the spritz!)

A humectant pulls water… from wherever there is more of it. Humid air: it pulls water from the air into your hair ✓. Very dry air (harmattan, heated indoor winters): it can pull water OUT of your hair into the air ✗. In dry season, reduce or skip the glycerin in these recipes.

Your equipment (all already at home)

A bowl, a fork or mini-whisk, a clean spray bottle, a plastic cap (or freezer bag + towel), and clips to work in 4 sections.

The winning duoLog every experiment in your recipe journal (p. 17) and your wash-day tracker. Within a month you will know exactly what YOUR hair loves — no more random buying.

modules.title: Intense moisture mask
modules.html:
Deep masks · Recipe 1

Intense moisture mask

For: dry, rough hair that snaps without stretching.

⏲ 30-45 min🔄 1x/week🧊 Use the same day
Ingredients
  • ½ very ripe avocado, blended silky smooth
  • 2 tbsp honey
  • 2 tbsp olive oil
  • 2 tbsp plain yogurt (optional, for texture)
1

Blend everything into a lump-free cream (crucial for rinsing).

2

On washed, towel-dried hair, apply in 4 sections, ends to roots.

3

Cover with a plastic cap + warm towel. Leave 30–45 min.

4

Rinse with lukewarm water, finish with a stream of cool water.

5

Follow with your leave-in and seal (L.O.C.).

Low porosity

Add heat (heat cap or a properly warm towel) and leave 45 min — heat is what carries the treatment in.

High porosity

After rinsing, seal immediately with shea or castor oil to lock the water inside.

Schicgirl tipA mask does not replace daily hydration: it is your big weekly recharge — the spritz (p. 9) does the rest.

modules.title: Strength & softness mask
modules.html:
Deep masks · Recipe 2

Strength & softness mask

For: limp, overly stretchy hair that stretches and stretches… then breaks.

⏲ 20-30 min🔄 Max 1x/2 weeks🧊 Use the same day
Ingredients
  • 1 very ripe banana, blended ultra-smooth (blender, then strain if needed)
  • 3 tbsp plain yogurt
  • 1 tbsp honey
  • 1 tbsp melted coconut oil
1

Blend at length — the banana must disappear completely.

2

Apply to clean, damp hair, section by section.

3

Plastic cap, 20–30 min, no heat.

4

Rinse very thoroughly with lukewarm water.

5

Follow with a light moisturizing conditioner, then seal.

Low porosity

Use half the banana and leave only 20 min — your hair saturates fast.

High porosity

You can leave it 30 min and add 1 extra tbsp of yogurt.

Schicgirl tipSchicgirl honesty: kitchen proteins coat the surface (instant strength feel) but do not repair deeply like the hydrolyzed proteins in a dedicated treatment. If your hair is very damaged, invest in a real protein treatment — and use this recipe for maintenance.

modules.title: Shea-coconut repair mask
modules.html:
Deep masks · Recipe 3

Shea-coconut repair mask

For: damaged ends, hair broken by manipulation or dryness.

⏲ 45 min to overnight🔄 1x/week🧊 Keeps 1 month (clean jar, no water)
Ingredients
  • 3 tbsp shea butter, melted in a water bath
  • 1 tbsp coconut oil
  • 1 tbsp olive oil
  • 1 tsp honey
1

Melt the shea gently, add the oils off the heat, whisk into a cream.

2

Chill 10 min in the fridge, whisking twice: whipped-cream texture.

3

On DAMP hair (water hydrates — butter seals), apply mostly on mid-lengths and ends.

4

Leave 45 min under a cap, or overnight on the ends only.

5

Rinse lightly, or keep it in as a sealant depending on how much you used.

Low porosity

Go very light: a hazelnut-sized amount for the whole head, or hello cardboard hair.

High porosity

Be generous on the ends — exactly what your porosity needs.

Schicgirl tipThis water-free blend keeps very well: whip a small jar for the whole month.

modules.title: Shine & definition mask
modules.html:
Deep masks · Recipe 4

Shine & definition mask

For: dull hair, curls that no longer clump, after summer or product build-up.

⏲ 20 min🔄 1x/2 weeks🧊 Use the same day
Ingredients
  • 4 tbsp aloe vera gel
  • 2 tbsp olive oil
  • 1 tbsp honey
  • Final rinse: 1 tbsp apple cider vinegar in 250 ml cool water
1

Mix aloe + oil + honey into a smooth gel.

2

Apply to clean, damp hair, gently scrunching the curls.

3

Leave 20 min under a cap, rinse with lukewarm water.

4

Finish with the vinegar rinse — do NOT rinse again; the smell vanishes as hair dries.

5

Dry by plopping with a cotton t-shirt.

Low porosity

The vinegar rinse is your best friend: it flattens the stubborn cuticles that dull your hair.

High porosity

Double the olive oil and seal afterwards — shine also comes from closed cuticles.

Schicgirl tipCoily shine does not look like straight-hair shine — look for sheen (a soft glow), not a mirror.

modules.title: Rice water protein rinse
modules.html:
Deep masks · Recipe 5

Rice water protein rinse

For: limp, over-moisturised hair that lacks strength or slip.

⏲ 20 min🔄 every 2–3 weeks🧊 use fresh
Ingredients
  • ½ cup uncooked rice
  • 2 cups water
  • optional: 1 drop peppermint oil or a citrus peel (for the smell)
1

Rinse the rice, then soak in the water 30 min (or lightly ferment 12–24 h at room temp for a stronger rinse — stop at a faintly sour smell).

2

Strain the cloudy water into a bottle. If fermented, dilute 1:1 with plain water.

3

On freshly washed hair, pour through and massage gently 2–3 min.

4

Leave 5–10 min maximum — this is protein, don’t overdo it.

5

Rinse thoroughly, then deep-condition with a moisture mask (this step is essential).

Low porosity

Your hair grabs protein fast — 5 min max, once a month is plenty.

High porosity

Loves the extra strength — up to every 2 weeks, always follow with moisture.

Schicgirl tip Rice water is protein. If hair feels stiff or straw-like afterwards, you’ve had too much — skip it for a month and focus on moisture.

modules.title: Banana & honey elasticity mask
modules.html:
Deep masks · Recipe 6

Banana & honey elasticity mask

For: brittle hair that snaps instead of stretching; softening after a protein treatment.

⏲ 30 min🔄 1x / 1–2 weeks🧊 same day
Ingredients
  • 1 very ripe banana (blended perfectly smooth — no lumps!)
  • 1 tbsp honey
  • 1 tbsp coconut or olive oil
  • 2 tbsp water or aloe juice
1

Blend the banana with the liquids until totally silky — un-blended bits are a nightmare to rinse, so blend twice.

2

Apply to damp hair, roots to ends, focusing on the mid-lengths.

3

Cover with a cap and leave 30 min.

4

Rinse very well under running water (be patient here).

5

Follow with your usual conditioner.

Low porosity

Add gentle heat and keep the oil light.

High porosity

Swap the water for aloe juice and seal afterwards — banana + aloe boosts elasticity.

Schicgirl tip Always blend, never mash — un-blended banana can dry into the hair. Strain through a sieve if you’re unsure.

modules.title: Daily hydrating spritz
modules.html:
Leave-ins & sprays · Recipe 7

Daily hydrating spritz

For: the foundation of EVERY Type 4 routine — morning and evening hydration.

⏲ 2 min/day🔄 Daily🧊 1 week in the fridge
Ingredients
  • 200 ml mineral or filtered water
  • 50 ml aloe vera juice
  • 1 tsp vegetable glycerin (SKIP in very dry season — see p. 4)
  • 1 tsp light oil (jojoba or olive)
1

Pour everything into a clean spray bottle, shake hard.

2

Morning and/or evening: mist the lengths (no need to soak).

3

Scrunch or smooth depending on your style, then seal the ends with a drop of oil.

4

Shake before EVERY use (the oil floats up).

5

Keep in the fridge between uses; make a fresh batch weekly.

Low porosity

Halve the oil — too much grease on low porosity = sticky hair.

High porosity

Mist more generously and seal systematically afterwards (shea or castor on the ends).

Schicgirl tipTHE recipe to adopt first. If you remember one thing from this book: water on your hair every day, sealed.

modules.title: Homemade hair milk
modules.html:
Leave-ins & sprays · Recipe 8

Homemade hair milk

For: a creamy leave-in after washing, or the “serious dryness” days.

⏲ 10 min to prepare🔄 After each wash🧊 5 days in the fridge
Ingredients
  • 100 ml warm water or aloe juice
  • 1 tbsp melted shea butter
  • 1 tsp olive oil
  • ½ tsp honey
1

Melt the shea in a water bath with the oil.

2

Pour in the warm water in a thin stream WHILE whisking non-stop (like mayonnaise).

3

Add the honey, whisk again — you get a light milk.

4

Apply to damp hair after washing, section by section.

5

Keep in the fridge and use within 5 days.

Low porosity

Use it on wash days only — the daily spritz covers the rest.

High porosity

Perfect daily for you if your hair drinks fast: spritz in the morning, milk at night.

Schicgirl tipIf it separates, that is normal (no industrial emulsifier): shake or re-whisk and it comes back.

modules.title: 2nd / 3rd day refresh
modules.html:
Leave-ins & sprays · Recipe 9

2nd / 3rd day refresh

For: waking up a wash&go, twist-out or braids without starting over.

⏲ 5 min🔄 Depends on style🧊 1 week in the fridge
Ingredients
  • 150 ml water
  • 2 tbsp of your usual leave-in (or the milk, p. 10)
  • 1 tsp aloe vera gel
1

Mix in a spray bottle — a very light milky texture.

2

In the morning, mist lightly, focusing on flattened areas.

3

Scrunch the curls head-down for 30 seconds.

4

Smooth frizz with a drop of oil in your palms.

5

Do NOT saturate — damp, not soaked, or you lose the definition.

Low porosity

Two sprays are enough — more water = instant shrinkage comeback.

High porosity

Add a drop of oil to the mix; your lengths will thank you.

Schicgirl tipA failed refresh usually means too much product, not too little. Lightness always wins.

modules.title: Aloe & rose daily mist
modules.html:
Leave-ins & sprays · Recipe 10

Aloe & rose daily mist

For: quick daily moisture and refreshing between wash days; low-porosity friendly.

⏲ 5 min🔄 daily as needed🧊 keeps ~1 week (fridge)
Ingredients
  • 2 tbsp pure aloe vera juice or gel
  • ½ cup rose water (or distilled water)
  • 1 tsp vegetable glycerine
  • 1 tsp light oil (jojoba / grapeseed)
1

Add everything to a spray bottle and shake well.

2

Mist lightly onto hair (damp, not soaking) and scrunch or smooth in.

3

Follow with a pea of leave-in on the ends if needed.

4

Store in the fridge and shake before each use.

5

Make small batches — no preservative, so use within a week.

Low porosity

Cut the oil to a few drops; aloe + glycerine absorb well on your hair.

High porosity

Add a little more oil, or mist then seal with a cream to hold the water in.

Schicgirl tip In very dry or cold air, drop the glycerine — it can pull moisture out of hair when the air is drier than your strands.

modules.title: Flaxseed curl gel
modules.html:
Leave-ins & sprays · Recipe 11

Flaxseed curl gel

For: soft hold, definition and moisture without crunch; wash-and-gos and twist-outs.

⏲ 20 min + cooling🔄 make fresh, ~1 week (fridge)🧊 styling
Ingredients
  • ¼ cup whole flaxseeds (linseeds)
  • 2 cups water
  • optional: 1 tsp aloe, a few drops of oil, 1 drop essential oil
1

Simmer the flaxseeds + water, stirring, until frothy and it coats the spoon like light egg-white (7–10 min). Don’t over-boil or it turns to glue.

2

Strain immediately through a fine sieve or nylon stocking while hot (it thickens as it cools).

3

Once cooled, stir in the aloe / oil.

4

Apply to soaked, conditioned hair in sections, then style as usual.

5

Keep in the fridge; toss it if it smells off.

Low porosity

Make a thinner gel (strain in a little more water) so it doesn’t just sit on top.

High porosity

Add a little oil or butter over the gel to lock in definition and stop frizz.

Schicgirl tip Stir a spoon of your favourite conditioner into the cooled gel for extra slip — “flax custard”.

modules.title: Detangling pre-poo
modules.html:
Pre-poos & oils · Recipe 12

Detangling pre-poo

For: protecting your hair BEFORE shampoo and turning detangling into a formality.

⏲ 30 min to overnight🔄 Every wash day🧊 Oil blends keep for months
Ingredients
  • 2 tbsp coconut oil
  • 2 tbsp olive oil
1

On DRY hair, the night before or 30 min before washing, split into 4–6 sections.

2

Apply the blend section by section, ends to roots.

3

FINGER-detangle as you go — knots melt in the oil.

4

Braid or twist each finished section.

5

Cover (cap), let it sit, then wash section by section without fully undoing them.

Low porosity

30 min is enough — beyond that, the oil mostly sits on the surface for you.

High porosity

A full night is ideal: the coconut oil has time to penetrate and limits wash-day breakage.

Schicgirl tipCoconut oil is one of the few oils proven to penetrate the strand and reduce protein loss during washing. This is THE highest-return anti-breakage step in your routine.

modules.title: Strengthening oil bath
modules.html:
Pre-poos & oils · Recipe 13

Strengthening oil bath

For: breakage phases, fragile ends, heavily handled hair (styles, manipulation).

⏲ 1 h to overnight🔄 1x/week as a course, else 1x/month🧊 Keeps for months (clean bottle)
Ingredients
  • 1 tbsp castor oil
  • 2 tbsp coconut or olive oil
  • Optional: 1 tbsp jojoba to thin it out
1

Blend the oils (castor alone is too thick).

2

Warm the bottle under hot water for 2 min — never boiling.

3

Apply to lengths and ends; massage the scalp 3 min if you like.

4

Leave 1 h under a cap (or overnight, satin pillowcase recommended).

5

Wash as usual — allow 2 gentle shampoos if needed.

Low porosity

Prefer 1 h with gentle heat over a full night — more effective, less residue.

High porosity

Overnight suits you well; even seal a micro-dose daily on the ends.

Schicgirl tipStraight talk: no oil makes hair grow faster. But less breakage = more length KEPT. That is the real castor-oil secret.

modules.title: Soothing scalp oil
modules.html:
Pre-poos & oils · Recipe 14

Soothing scalp oil

For: light itching, tightness, dry scalp under protective styles.

⏲ 5 min🔄 2-3x/week🧊 Keeps 3 months (opaque bottle)
Ingredients
  • 4 tbsp jojoba oil (or olive)
  • 4–8 drops tea tree essential oil (1-2% max)
  • Optional: 2 drops peppermint for the cooling effect
1

Mix in a thin-nozzle applicator bottle (very handy under braids).

2

PATCH TEST 24 h — mandatory before first use.

3

Drop directly onto the scalp, part by part.

4

Massage 2-3 minutes with fingertip pads (never nails).

5

No need to rinse.

Low porosity

Your scalp already makes sebum that circulates poorly: massage more, oil less.

High porosity

Use without excess: a smothered scalp itches too. 2-3x/week max.

Schicgirl tipIntense itching, plaques, thick flakes? That is no longer DIY territory — see a professional, and read our Goodbye Dandruff guide. Honesty first.

modules.title: Rosemary & peppermint scalp oil
modules.html:
Pre-poos & oils · Recipe 15

Rosemary & peppermint scalp oil

For: itchy or sluggish scalp; supporting a healthy environment for growth.

⏲ 30 min – overnight🔄 1–2x / week🧊 pre-wash
Ingredients
  • 3 tbsp carrier oil (jojoba / castor / olive)
  • 3–4 sprigs fresh rosemary OR 4 drops rosemary essential oil
  • 2 drops peppermint essential oil
  • optional: 1 vitamin-E capsule
1

Fresh rosemary: warm it gently in the oil ~10 min (don’t fry), cool and strain. Essential oils: just mix into the carrier.

2

Part the hair and apply to the scalp with your fingertips (not the lengths).

3

Massage 3–5 min — this is the part that actually matters (circulation).

4

Leave 30 min to overnight (protect your pillow).

5

Wash out on wash day as normal.

Low porosity

Use a light carrier (jojoba / grapeseed) so it rinses out clean.

High porosity

Castor oil works well and doubles as a sealant for your ends afterwards.

Schicgirl tip Rosemary has promising evidence for the scalp, but it’s a helper, not magic — consistency and a healthy routine do the heavy lifting. Always dilute essential oils; never apply them neat.

modules.title: Rhassoul cleansing mask
modules.html:
Gentle cleansers · Recipe 16

Rhassoul cleansing mask

For: washing without stripping, in the weeks when your hair is more dry than dirty.

⏲ 20 min🔄 Every other wash🧊 Mix fresh — wet clay does not keep
Ingredients
  • 4 tbsp rhassoul clay powder
  • Warm water or aloe juice (to a yogurt texture)
  • 1 tsp honey
  • 1 tsp olive oil
1

Mix the rhassoul with the liquid to a yogurt-like paste (non-metal bowl preferred).

2

Add honey and oil.

3

On very wet hair, apply part by part on the scalp, then the lengths.

4

Massage gently 3 min — no foam is normal; it still cleans.

5

Leave max 10 min WITHOUT letting it dry, rinse very thoroughly.

Low porosity

Rhassoul will suit you particularly well — it leaves no greasy film.

High porosity

Add 1 extra tbsp of oil to the mix and seal well afterwards.

Schicgirl tipAlternate: rhassoul one wash, gentle shampoo the next. Hair stays clean, sebum stays in peace.

modules.title: Boosted co-wash
modules.html:
Gentle cleansers · Recipe 17

Boosted co-wash

For: refreshing between shampoos, especially under styles or with regular workouts.

⏲ 10 min🔄 Between shampoos🧊 Mix fresh
Ingredients
  • 4 tbsp of your simplest conditioner (silicone-free preferred)
  • 1 tsp olive oil
  • 1 tsp honey
1

Mix the three ingredients in a bowl.

2

On very wet hair, apply to the scalp first.

3

Massage the scalp for 3 real minutes — the massage cleans, not the foam.

4

Pull through the lengths, finger-detangle.

5

Rinse thoroughly.

Low porosity

Co-washing can weigh your hair down fast — keep a real gentle shampoo every other wash.

High porosity

Excellent for you: co-wash cleans without reopening the door to dryness.

Schicgirl tipCo-washing does not replace shampoo forever: if the scalp itches or gets greasy fast, that is the signal to go back to a classic wash.

modules.title: Apple cider vinegar rinse
modules.html:
Gentle cleansers · Recipe 18

Apple cider vinegar rinse

For: product buildup, dullness, an itchy flaky scalp, and resetting pH.

⏲ 5 min🔄 every 2–4 weeks🧊 after shampoo
Ingredients
  • 1–2 tbsp apple cider vinegar (with “the mother”)
  • 1 cup (250 ml) water
  • optional: 1 tsp honey, or brew with chamomile tea
1

Mix the ACV into the water — always dilute, never apply it neat.

2

After cleansing, pour slowly over the hair and scalp.

3

Massage the scalp gently 1–2 min.

4

Leave 1–2 min, then rinse with cool water (or leave in if very diluted).

5

Deep-condition afterwards.

Low porosity

Great for lifting the buildup that weighs you down — keep it monthly and moisturise after.

High porosity

Closes the cuticle for shine and less frizz; you can use it a touch more often.

Schicgirl tip The smell vanishes completely once hair is dry. If your scalp stings, you used too much ACV — dilute more next time.

modules.title: My recipe journal
modules.html:
Your tool

My recipe journal

Every time you try or tweak a recipe, log it here. Within a few weeks you will own YOUR personal recipe book — the one that works on YOUR hair. Photocopy this page as often as you need.

My recipe #___
Name
For which need?
Ingredients & amounts
Time / method
Result ★★★★★
Again? Y / N — and what to change
My recipe #___
Name
For which need?
Ingredients & amounts
Time / method
Result ★★★★★
Again? Y / N — and what to change

modules.title: “My hair is doing X” → which recipe?
modules.html:
Your cheat sheet

“My hair is doing X” → which recipe?

Stick this page on your bathroom wall. Symptom on the left, answer on the right.

Your hair is telling you…Your answer
Dry, rough hair that snapsIntense moisture mask (p. 5) + Daily spritz (p. 9)
Limp hair that stretches then breaksStrength & softness mask (p. 6) — then re-moisturize
Damaged ends, breakage on the lengthsShea-coconut mask (p. 7) + Oil bath (p. 13)
Dull hair, undefined curlsShine mask + vinegar rinse (p. 8)
Everyday drynessSpritz (p. 9) morning/evening + sealing
Nightmare detanglingDetangling pre-poo the night before (p. 12)
Scalp tightness or light itchingSoothing scalp oil (p. 14) + massages
Wash day dries you outRhassoul (p. 15) alternated + co-wash (p. 16)
Flat twist-out on day 2Refresh (p. 11) — light, head down
What about rice water?

We get asked all the time. Honest verdict: some women love it, others end up with stiff, brittle hair (a “too much rigidity” effect close to protein overload). If you want to try: rinse water fermented 24 h max, once every 2 weeks maximum, and stop immediately if your hair stiffens. Never if your hair already snaps “stiff”.

The 2-week ruleWhatever you test: 2 weeks, one new thing only, and your tracker sheet filled in. That is how recipes turn into results.
